desc: Makassar is South Sulawesi’s main coastal hub, combining a busy urban waterfront with easy access to island-hopping routes and inland highlands. Travel quality hinges on rainfall seasonality: the driest mid-year months are easiest for boat days, while wet-season months bring greener landscapes but higher disruption risk.
hls: Sunset promenade culture along Losari Beach
Historic Fort Rotterdam and Bugis-Makassar maritime heritage
Seafood-focused food scene with late-night local warungs
Gateway to Spermonde Islands snorkeling and sandbars
Practical launch point for South Sulawesi overland routes
Strong value outside Indonesian and school-holiday peaks

todo.name: Losari waterfront at golden hour
todo.desc: Walk the promenade near sunset, then sample local snacks and street-food stalls after dark.

todo.name: Fort Rotterdam + museum stop
todo.desc: Visit the Dutch-era fort complex and museum collections to understand regional trading history.

todo.name: Paotere harbor boat scene
todo.desc: Go in the morning to see traditional pinisi schooners and working harbor activity.

todo.name: Spermonde island day trip
todo.desc: Charter or join a small-group boat trip for reef stops and sandbank downtime in calmer sea windows.

todo.name: Trans Studio or rainy-day fallback
todo.desc: Keep one weather-resilient activity ready for heavy-rain afternoons in monsoon months.

todo.name: Coto Makassar food crawl
todo.desc: Do an evening tasting route focused on coto, konro, and grilled seafood specialties.

wifi.note: Business hotels and modern cafés are generally workable, but consistency drops during storms or outside central corridors.

prac.fltNote: No direct UK services; most itineraries route via Jakarta, Bali, Kuala Lumpur, or Singapore before a domestic leg to UPG. Best fares are usually outside July-August and late-December travel spikes.
prac.lang: Indonesian (Bahasa Indonesia); local languages include Makassarese and Buginese
prac.visa: Indonesia entry options (eVOA/visa-free by nationality) vary by passport and purpose; confirm current rules before departure.
alerts: November to April can bring heavy rain and choppy seas that affect island trips.
Domestic flights can retime at short notice; leave buffer before international connections.
Heat/humidity are year-round—plan outdoor walks early or late and hydrate consistently.

sweet: June to September is usually the easiest Makassar window for boat trips and lower rain disruption, with May/October as workable shoulders.
